title: Highly phenylated poly(aryl ether phthalazine)s
abstract:
Highly Phenylated Poly(ary1 ether phtha1azine)s 
EDMUND ELCE and ALLAN S. HAY* 
Department of Chemistry, McGill University, 801 Sherbrooke Street W., Montreal, Quebec H3A 2K6, Canada 
SYNOPSIS 
Two series of novel amorphous poly(ary1 ether phtha1azine)s have been prepared via an 
intramolecular ring closure reaction of poly(ary1 ether ketone)s (PAEKs) with hydrazine 
monohydrate. Fluorinated PAEKs, which display solubility in solvents incorporating a 
ketone functionality such as acetone or ethyl acetate, were converted to poly(ary1 ether 
phtha1azine)s to observe if these polymers would display similar solubility characteristics. 
The poly(ary1 ether phtha1azine)s have glass transition temperatures in the range of 278- 
320°C and show 5% weight loss points greater than 500°C in air and nitrogen atmospheres. 
The fluorinated poly(ary1 ether phtha1azine)s were not soluble in ketonic solvents. A series 
of poly(ary1 ether phtha1azine)s incorporating pendant 2-naphthalenyl moieties has been 
prepared in an attempt to produce amorphous, thermally stable polymers with high glass 
transition temperatures. The polymers have glass transition temperatures in the range of 
287-334°C and show 5% weight loss points greater than 500°C in air and nitrogen atmo- 
spheres. Poly(ary1 ether phtha1azine)s undergo an exothermic reaction above the glass 
transition temperature. The major product of this reaction is a rearrangement of the 
phthalazine moieties to quiazoline moieties, however some crosslinking of the polymers 
occurs. Cured samples of the poly(ary1 ether phtha1azine)s show a small increase in the 
polymer T, and are insoluble in all solvents tested. 0 
1996 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. 
Keywords: poly(ary1 ether ketone)s poly(ary1 ether phtha1azine)s 9 fluorinated high Tg 
INTRODUCTION 
Thermoplastic polymers incorporating heterocyclic 
groups in the polymer architecture have become in- 
creasingly important because they exhibit many de- 
sirable properties.' The success of commercial ma- 
terials such as polyimides, polybenzimidizoles, and 
poly(phenylquinoxa1ine)s has spurred research into 
polymers containing novel heterocyclic rings in an 
effort to optimize properties for specific applica- 
tions.' Many commercial heterocyclic polymers suf- 
fer from low solubility due to their highly rigid, ar- 
omatic backbones, and thus are difficult to process. 
This difficulty has been overcome by introducing 
flexible linkages into the polymer backbone. Aryl 
ether linkages have been used successfully to in- 
crease the processsability of heterocyclic polymers 
* To whom all correspondence should be addressed. 
Journal of Polymer Science: Part A Polymer Chemistry, Vol. 34,1897-1905 (1996) 
0 
1996 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. 
CCC OSS7-624X/96/101897-09 
generally with some sacrifice in thermo-oxidative 
~tability.~ 
One heterocyclic group which has received little 
attention is the phthalazine moiety. Polymers in- 
corporating the phthalazine moiety have been pre- 
pared via several different methods. Phthalazine has 
been polymerized in an oxidative electrochemical 
polymerization to give high molecular weight 
poly(phtha1azine) in an attempt to prepare a con- 
ducting organic p01ymer.~ 
Amorphous poly( aryl ether phtha1azine)s have 
been reported by Singh and Hay.3 The phthalazine 
polymers were prepared following two different pro- 
cedures. Poly(ary1 ether ketone)s containing an or- 
tho-diketone moiety were reacted with an excess of 
hydrazine monohydrate to give poly(ary1 ether 
phtha1azine)s and these polymers were also prepared 
from fluorinated monomers, which used the phthal- 
azine moiety as an activating group, by nucleophi- 
lic displacement polymerization with commercial 
bisphenols. A series of amorphous rigid rod 
poly(phtha1azine)s prepared via a carbon-carbon 
1897 
 1898 
ELCE AND HAY 
coupling reaction of chlorinated monomers has also 
been rep~rted.~ 
We have reported that the introduction of fluorine 
into poly(ary1 ether ketone)s caused a reduction in 
crystallinity of semicrystalline polymers and en- 
hanced the solubility of amorphous polymers, as 
compared to isostructural, nonfluorinated poly- 
m e r ~ . ~ ' ~  
We have continued this research to deter- 
mine if this behavior is also manifested in poly(ary1 
ether phtha1azine)s. A second objective of this work 
was to prepare amorphous thermoplastic polymers 
with high glass transition temperatures which could 
be used in load bearing applications at high tem- 
peratures. The strategy employed to accomplish this 
objective was to incorporate large aromatic pendant 
groups into known polymer architectures. 
In this article we report the synthesis of two series 
of poly(ary1 ether phtha1azine)s via a ring closure 
reaction of poly(ary1 ether ketone)s polymers with 
an excess of hydrazine monohydrate. The synthesis 
of the polymers and their properties are discussed. 
EXPERIMENTAL 
General Methods 
Melting points were recorded on a Fisher-Johns 
melting point apparatus. The 'H- and 13C-NMR data 
were collected using a Varian Unity 500 MHz in- 
strument and referenced to tetramethylsilane 
(TMS). The "F-NMR data were collected on a Var- 
ian Unity 500 MHz instrument and were referenced 
to fluorobenzene. Inherent viscosity data was ob- 
tained using chloroform solutions with a concentra- 
tion of 0.5 g/dL in a calibrated 1 %, (176) Ubbelohde 
dilution viscometer at 25°C in a controlled temper- 
ature water bath. The water bath temperature was 
controlled using a Julabo (model type, PC) heater. 
Apparent molecular weights were determined by 
gel permeation chromatography (GPC) using a cal- 
ibration curve constructed with monodisperse poly- 
styrene standards. The polymers were analyzed with 
a Waters 510 HPLC with a UV detector (A = 254 
nm) and four ultra-styragel columns (lo5, lo4, 500, 
and 100 A) in series. The solvent used was chloro- 
form/THF (90/10). 
Polymer samples for thermal characterization 
were dried at 200°C in a vacuum oven (1 mm Hg) 
for 18 h before use. The polymer was pressed into 
pellets under 5 tons of pressure using a KBr pellet 
die. Glass transition temperatures (T,) 
were mea- 
sured using a Seiko 220 Differential Scanning Cal- 
orimeter (DSC) at a heating rate of 20"C/min under 
a nitrogen atmosphere. The T, was taken as the 
midpoint of the change in slope of the baseline. All 
values reported are from the second scan. 
Thermogravimetric analyses (TGA) were ob- 
tained using a Seiko 220 TG/DTA instrument. The 
5% weight loss points were recorded using a heating 
rate of 10"C/min under atmospheres of nitrogen and 
air. All polymer samples were dried under a nitrogen 
atmosphere at the Tg 
(as determined by DSC) for 1 
hour before performing TGA measurements. 
Young's moduli were recorded using a Seiko 
TMA/SS12O instrument. The polymer films used 
for these measurements were prepared as follows: 
150 mg of polymer was dissolved in 3 mL of chlo- 
roform. The films were cast from a chloroform so- 
lution onto glass plates using circular glass molds 1 
in. in diam. The chloroform was allowed to evaporate 
at room temperature and the films were dried at 
85°C for 18 h in a forced air oven and under vacuum 
(1 mm Hg) at 100°C for 18 h. 
Elemental analyses were performed by Galbraith 
Laboratories, Knoxville, TN. 
Materials 
Hydrazine monohydrate (Aldrich) and 1,4-dioxane 
(BDH) were used as received. 
Monomer Synthesis 
1,2-Bis(4-fluorobenzoyl)-3,4,5,6-tetrakis(4-fluoro- 
pheny1)benzene 1aCompound l a  was prepared as 
described in the literat~re.~ 
1,4,5,6,7,8-Hexakis(4-fluorophenyl)phthalazine 2 
A 100 mL three neck flask was charged with 1,2- 
bis( 4-fluorobenzoyl)-3,4,5,6-tetrakis(4-fluoro- 
pheny1)benzene l a  (1.00 g, 1.43 mmol) and 1,4- 
dioxane (200 mL). The flask was placed in an ice 
bath and the solution was cooled for 30 min. A ni- 
trogen gas inlet was attached and the flask was 
flushed with N2 gas. Hydrazine monohydrate was 
added to the flask dropwise, over a period of 30 min. 
A reflux condenser was attached and the solution 
was refluxed for 18 h. The solution was cooled and 
the solvent was removed under reduced pressure. 
The crude solid was adsorbed onto silica gel and 
subject to flash chromatography (petroleum ether/ 
ethyl acetate 2/1). The crude solid recovered from 
the column was recrystallized from xylenes to yield 
2 (0.71, 71%) as a yellow powder. mp 332-333°C:

Department of Chemistry, McGill University, 801 Sherbrooke Street W., Montreal, Quebec H3A 2K6, Canada
Two series of novel amorphous poly(ary1 ether phtha1azine)s have been prepared via an
intramolecular ring closure reaction of poly(ary1 ether ketone)s (PAEKs) with hydrazine
monohydrate. Fluorinated PAEKs, which display solubility in solvents incorporating a
ketone functionality such as acetone or ethyl acetate, were converted to poly(ary1 ether
phtha1azine)s to observe if these polymers would display similar solubility characteristics.
The poly(ary1 ether phtha1azine)s have glass transition temperatures in the range of 278-
320°C and show 5% weight loss points greater than 500°C in air and nitrogen atmospheres.
The fluorinated poly(ary1 ether phtha1azine)s were not soluble in ketonic solvents. A series
of poly(ary1 ether phtha1azine)s incorporating pendant 2-naphthalenyl moieties has been
prepared in an attempt to produce amorphous, thermally stable polymers with high glass
transition temperatures. The polymers have glass transition temperatures in the range of
287-334°C and show 5% weight loss points greater than 500°C in air and nitrogen atmo-
spheres. Poly(ary1 ether phtha1azine)s undergo an exothermic reaction above the glass
transition temperature. The major product of this reaction is a rearrangement of the
phthalazine moieties to quiazoline moieties, however some crosslinking of the polymers
occurs. Cured samples of the poly(ary1 ether phtha1azine)s show a small increase in the
polymer T, and are insoluble in all solvents tested. 0

Poly(ary1 ether ketone)s 6a-f and 7a-f, endcapped
with 4-phenylphenol, were prepared as described in
A general procedure is given for the ring closure of
poly(ary1 ether ketone)s 6a-f and 7a-e to the
poly(phtha1azine)s 8a-f and 9a-f with hydrazine
monohydrate using polymer 8a as an e ~ a m p l e . ~
Ring closures of the poly(ary1 ether ketone)s were
conducted following the procedure described by
Singh.5 A 500 mL round bottom flask was charged
with poly(ary1 ether ketone) 6a (l.OOg, 1.13 mmol)
and 1,4-dioxane (250 mL). The flask was placed in
an ice bath and the solution was cooled for 30 min.
A nitrogen gas inlet was attached and the flask was
flushed with N2 gas. Hydrazine monohydrate (0.113
g, 2.26 mmol) was added to the flask dropwise over
a period of 5 min. The solution was stirred in the
ice bath for 1 h and then allowed to warm to room
temperature. A reflux condenser was attached and
the solution was refluxed for 18 h. The solution was
cooled and the solvent was removed under reduced
pressure. The resulting crude poly( aryl ether
Structures of ketone and phthalazine mono-
phthalazine) 8a was dissolved in hot chloroform
(100 mL), condensed, and precipitated into meth-
anol/water (200/100 mL). The solid polymer was
recovered by filtration and dried for 12 h at 100°C
1,4,5,6,7,8-Hexakis ( 4-fluorophenyl) phthalazine 2
was prepared following the procedure of Singh.3 The
synthesis of 1,2-bisbenzoyl-3,4,5,6-tetrakis
rophenyl ) benzene l a  was reported previ~usly.~
reaction of l a  with an excess of hydrazine mono-
hydrate was conducted for 18 h in refluxing 1,4-
dioxane (Scheme 1 ) . The crude product was purified
by flash column chromatography and then recrys-
tallized from xylenes giving 2 in 71% yield.
Poly (aryl ether phthalazine) s have been prepared
from monomer 4 (Fig. 1) with commercial bis-
phenols in refluxing NMP.3 To obtain high molec-
ular weight polymer, reaction times of 18-20 h were
required. The diketone monomer 3 (Fig. 1 ) , used
in the synthesis of 4, can be polymerized to high
molecular weight polymer in 4 h., We have reported
that polymerization of monomer l a  (Fig. 1 ) with
commercial bisphenols in sulfolane at 235°C gave
high molecular weight polymers after 3 h, however,
branching occured at pendant phenyl ring B via the
displacement of a fluorine atom by a phenate anion.7
The reactivity of a fluorine atom to displacement
by a phenate anion can be estimated using "F-
NMR.8 The six fluorine atoms of monomer 2 (Fig.
1) were evaluated in this manner and compared to
the data of l a  (Table I). The fluorine of phenyl ring
A is the expected site of polymerization of l a  and
2. The difference in chemical shifts for the phenyl
"F Resonances of the Fluorine Atoms of la
a All spectra are referenced internally to fluorobenzene
ring A fluorines of l a  (-104.3 ppm) and 2 (-110.8
ppm) indicate a significant difference in reactivity.
The long reaction time required to polymerize 4
confirms that the phthalazine moiety is a much
weaker activating group than the ketone m ~ i e t y . ~
much smaller difference is observed for the chemical
shifts of the fluorines of phenyl rings B and C of l a
(-113.8, -114.7 ppm) and 2 (-115.8, -117.0 ppm).
It was believed that the longer reaction times and
high temperatures necessary to synthesize high mo-
lecular weight polymer from 2 and commercial bis-
phenols would result in displacement of fluorine in
the pendant phenyl rings with concomitant branch-
ing. The synthesis of poly (aryl ether phthalazine) s
was therefore accomplished by first preparing the
poly(ary1 ether  ketone)^ and then ring closing the
polymers with hydrazine monohydrate. The ring
closure reaction with hydrazine monohydrate is vi-
able because it has been shown to be quantitative
and does not cause any scission of the polymer
Monomers l a  and l b  were polymerized with
commercial bisphenols in DMAc at 155°C in the
presence of excess K&03 (Scheme 2) .7 A small ex-
cess of the dihalide monomer was used to control
the molecular weight and the polymers 6a-f and
7a-e were endcapped by the addition of 4-phenyl-
phenol once high molecular weight polymer was ob-
tained. All PAEKs were isolated in greater than 95%
yield. PAEK 7 f  could not be prepared due to an
electron transfer reaction that occurs between lb
and hydroquinone 5 f .' The PAEKs 6a-f and 7a-
e were dissolved in 1,4-dioxane and an excess of hy-
drazine monohydrate was added. The polymers were
refluxed for 18 hours to produce poly (aryl ether
phthalazine ) s 8a-f and 9a-e ( Scheme 3 ) in quan-
titative yield. The ring closure reaction can be mon-
itored by 13C-NMR, a complete reaction is indicated
by the disappearance of the signal of the carbonyl
carbon of the benzoyl moiety (Fig. 2 )  .3
The physical and mechanical properties of poly-
mers 8a-f and 9a-e appear in Table 11. Polymers
8a-f have inherent viscosities of 0.62-0.89 dL/g.
The glass transition temperatures (T,) are in the
range of 278-320°C, and all of the polymers exhibit
5% weight loss points in air and nitrogen atmo-
spheres exceeding 510°C. A comparison of this data
with that of PAEKs 6a-f (Table 111) shows that
ring closure of the polymers to poly(ary1 ether
phthalazine ) s has resulted in a dramatic change in
the physical properties. Polymers 8a-f show sig-
nificantly higher inherent viscosities and Tgs than
polymers 6a-f. The elimination of two flexible ke-
tone moieties per repeat unit results in a straight-
ening of the polymer backbone, increasing it's hy-
drodynamic volume and producing a much stiffer
observed in the thermo-oxidative stability of the
Polymers 8a-f were easily cast into transparent,
flexible yellow films from chloroform solutions. The
Young's moduli for polymers 8a-f are in the range
of 1.71-2.11 GPa at 25°C and 1.13-1.59 GPa at
200°C. These values are comparable to other engi-
neering thermoplastics. The Tgs of polymers 8a-f,
as measured from the tan 6, are somewhat lower
than the values measured by DSC due to mild me-
chanical deformations inherent in the testing
The presence of fluorine in PAEKs 6a-f was re-
ported to result in polymers which exhibited solu-
bility in organic solvents which contain a ketone
moiety, such as acetone, ethyl acetate and cyclo-
Isostructural nonfluorinated PAEKs
were not soluble in these solvents." It was thought
that the presence of fluorine in 8a-f would impart
solubility in ketonic solvents to the poly( aryl ether
phthalazine) s. However, polymers 8a-f were not
Physical Properties of PAEKs 6a-f
soluble in ketonic solvents, suggesting that the
flexible ketone moieties of 6a-f also play a role
in determining the solubility of the fluorinated
Polymers 9a-e have inherent viscosities of
0.54-0.85 dL/g. The polymer Tgs are in the range
of 287-334°C and all the polymers exhibit 5%
weight loss points in air and nitrogen atmospheres
exceeding 515°C. A comparison of the data for
polymers 9a-e with that of PAEKs 7a-e (Table
IV) shows that the formation of the phthalazine
moieties resulted in an increase in polymer vis-
cosity and Tg as a result of the straightening of
Table 11. Physical and Mechanical Properties of Polymers 8a-f and 9a-e
Table IV. Physical Properties of PAEKs 7a-e
Polymers 9a-e were easily cast into transparent,
flexible, yellow films from chloroform solutions. The
Young's moduli for polymers 9a-f are in the range
of 1.89-2.37 GPa at 25°C and 1.22-1.37 GPa at
200°C. These values are comparable to other engi-
neering thermoplastics. The Tgs of polymers 9a-f,
as measured from the tan 6, are somewhat lower
than the values measured by DSC due to mild me-
chanical deformations inherent in the testing
One of the objectives of incorporating the 2-
naphthalenyl moieties into the polymer architecture
was to produce an amorphous poly ( aryl ether
phthalazine) with a high Tg. Poly( aryl ether
phthalazine)~ with the structures 10a-c (Fig. 3 )
have been reported previously. Because the Tg of
polymers 10a-c increased as the number of pendant
aromatic groups increased, it was believed that fur-
ther increasing the size of the pendant aromatic
Structures and Tgs of poly(ary1 ether phthal-
Figure 4. Molecular models of compounds 4 and 1 1.
groups would result in an increase in the polymer
T,. A comparison of molecular models of 4 and 1 1,
the phthalazine compound that was used to prepare
polymers 10a-e via nucleophilic displacement po-
shows that a small increase in the
bulk of the molecule is attained when two pendant
phenyl rings are replaced with 2-naphthalenyl rings
(Fig. 4). No increase in the T, is observed when
comparing polymers 1Oc and 9a. The presence of
the bulky 2-naphthalenyl moieties does not result
in an increase of the polymer Tg.
The GPC data for polymers 8a-f and 9a-e are
presented in Table V. The high values of M ,
(>800,000) and M ,  (1,300,000) suggest that the

column with the injected solvent front as is com-
monly observed for rigid rod structures and that the
results do not reflect absolute M,, and Mw values.
All polymers show a monodisperse molecular weight
When poly (aryl ether phthalazine ) s were heated
in the DSC, under a nitrogen atmosphere, a large
exothermic peak was observed just above the poly-
(Fig. 5 ) .  The polymer sample loses approx-
imately 1-276 of its total weight at this time, which
suggests that substantial decomposition of the poly-
mer is not occurring. It has been found that when
1,4,5,6,7,8-hexaphenylphthalazine
an inert atmosphere, it underwent an exothermic
rearrangement reaction to give quinazoline 13 in
70% yield and an insoluble crosslinked material
.I2 This reaction has also been shown to
occur in poly( aryl ether phthalazine) s.12 Polymers
8a-f and 9a-e were cured at 350°C under a nitro-
gen atmosphere for 30 min. A comparison of the Tgs
of the polymers before and after curing appears in
Table VI. The T, of polymers 8a-f and 9a-e in-
creased on curing. The cured samples were tested
for solubility and were found to be insoluble in all
solvents. A small amount of swelling was evident
for some of the samples in chloroform. Both of these
observations suggest that a crosslinking reaction had
occurred. The efficiency of the crosslinking reaction
is considered to be poor based upon the small in-
crease in T, displayed by the polymers. The majority
of the phthalazine moieties in the polymer backbone
are thought to have undergone thermal rearrange-
ment to quinazoline moieties.12 Poly (aryl ether
quinazoline) 14 (Scheme 5) has been prepared from
quinazoline 13 and BPA 5a and the polymer is sol-
uble in a variety of solvents and has a Tg
Effect of Crosslinking on the T, of
Poly( aryl ether phthalazine) 1Oc has a Tg
These data support the suggestion that some cross-
linking occurs when the poly( aryl ether phthala-
zine) s underwent the thermal reaction. The mech-
anism of the thermal reaction is not understood.
Two series of poly (aryl ether phthalazine) s have
been prepared by the intramolecular ring closure of
poly (aryl ether ketone) s, containing an ortho-di-
ketone moiety, with an excess of hydrazine mono-
hydrate. The reaction was monitored with 13C-NMR
and found to be quantitative. The poly (aryl ether
phthalazine ) s display increased inherent viscosities
and T,s in comparison to the precursor PAEKs due
to straightening of the polymer backbone caused by
the elimination of two flexible ketone moieties per
The polymers undergo an exothermic reaction
of the polymers. Polymer samples were
cured at 350°C under nitrogen for 30 min and were
found to display increased glass transition temper-
atures and were insoluble in organic solvents. The
phthalazine moieties are believed to have undergone
